steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The hypothesis predicts that greater pressure fluctuations at the snout lead to better avoidance of obstructions in low visibility. To weaken it, a finding must show that higher pressure fluctuations do NOT produce better obstacle avoidance.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — a single counterexample fish: One fish colliding with an obstruction is anecdotal and lacks the comparative force needed to weaken the hypothesis. ✗
  • Choice B — intermediate-headed and wide-headed fish collide at the same rate: Intermediate-headed fish produce higher pressure fluctuations than wide-headed fish, so the hypothesis predicts they should avoid obstructions better. Equal collision rates directly contradict that prediction. ✓
  • Choice C — narrow-headed fish avoid obstructions more than wide-headed fish: This confirms the hypothesis rather than weakening it. ✗
  • Choice D — variation in pressure fluctuations among individuals of the same head shape: Individual variation within a group is unrelated to whether head shape and pressure fluctuations predict avoidance across groups. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
